ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Ice Kacang; closest match is shaved ice dessert (FOODON_03311737). Ice Kacang is a specific Southeast Asian variant with red beans, attap chee, grass jelly, coloured syrups, and condensed milk.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers ice-based desserts broadly; no specific Ice Kacang entry exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue. Used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Ice Kacang or equivalent shaved ice dessert category.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Ice Kacang is one bowl weighing approximately 350 g, comprising a generous mound of shaved ice over a base of red beans, attap chee, sweet corn, and grass jelly, drenched in coloured syrups and finished with condensed and evaporated milk.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient Composition of Common Singaporean and Malaysian Desserts
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Ice Kacang nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore food composition data for this dish. Values represent a typical assembled serving normalised to per-100 g basis.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson, Foster-Powell & Brand-Miller
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Ice Kacang exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is estimated by analogy to sweetened shaved ice / sugar syrup-based desserts with comparable carbohydrate composition (predominantly sucrose and glucose from syrups and condensed milk). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 58.8) / 100 = 41.2, rounded to 39.3 using available carbohydrate fraction. Estimation confidence: 0.70.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (15 mg per 100 g; ~52.5 mg per 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Ice Kacang is low in sodium and does not directly raise blood pressure; however, its high sugar load may contribute to metabolic dysfunction and insulin resistance, which are indirect risk factors for hypertension, so consumption should remain occasional.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~70) and high sugar content (14.5 g per 100 g; ~50.75 g per 350 g serving) from refined syrups and condensed milk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ice Kacang is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high simple sugar content and high GI, which will cause a rapid and significant postprandial blood glucose spike; if consumed, portion size should be strictly limited and syrups and condensed milk minimised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar intake (predominantly sucrose from syrups and condensed milk) linked to elevated triglycerides and adverse lipid profiles
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high free sugar content of Ice Kacang can elevate serum triglycerides and contribute to dyslipidaemia; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional indulgence and limit serving frequency to avoid cumulative metabolic harm.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar and artificial food colouring content in syrups may be pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals following an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern for rheumatoid arthritis should limit Ice Kacang consumption due to its high refined sugar content and the presence of artificial colourings in traditional syrups.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ice Kacang (also known as Ais Kacang, Air Batu Campur, or 红豆冰) is one of Singapore and Malaysia's most iconic cold desserts. The dish is assembled rather than cooked: a bowl is first layered with a base of cooked sweetened red beans (azuki), attap chee (palm seed jelly), sweet corn kernels, and grass jelly (cincau). A large mound of finely machine-shaved ice is then packed on top and generously drenched in brightly coloured sweet syrups — traditionally red rose syrup, green pandan syrup, and dark brown gula melaka (palm sugar) syrup. The dessert is finished with a drizzle of sweetened condensed milk and evaporated milk, and may optionally be topped with roasted peanuts, red bean paste, or a scoop of ice cream or durian. The texture is a contrast of icy, fluffy shaved ice melting into the chewy, soft toppings below. The flavour profile is intensely sweet, floral, and caramel-like from the syrups, with a creamy richness from the condensed milk. Nutritionally, Ice Kacang is high in simple sugars (14.5 g per 100 g) and refined carbohydrates (16.8 g per 100 g), with a high glycemic index of approximately 70, making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose. It is low in fat (0.5 g per 100 g), low in protein (0.8 g per 100 g), and very low in sodium (15 mg per 100 g). A standard 350 g serving provides approximately 263 kcal. The dish is a beloved street food and hawker centre staple across Singapore and Malaysia, particularly popular as a cooling treat in the hot and humid tropical climate.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Ice Kacang?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore food composition data for Ice Kacang, an exact match for this dish. Core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are complete. GI is estimated by analogy to comparable sugar-syrup-based shaved ice desserts from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, with confidence 0.70 for GI only.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, cholesterol, and moisture data for assembled Ice Kacang are not reported in the HPB Singapore food composition database as of 2024. 2. saturated_fat_g (0.3 g per 100 g) is estimated from the condensed milk and evaporated milk contribution; not explicitly reported in the HPB source and should be verified if precision is required. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero / naturally zero): Ice Kacang contains no vitamin-D-contributing ingredients; this is consistent with the HPB source reporting 0 for this nutrient. 4. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database framework for sugar-syrup-based desserts; no direct GI measurement for Ice Kacang was found. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (70 × 58.8) / 100 ≈ 41.2 (reported as 39.3 using available carbohydrate fraction). 5. Portions in the input contained three identical duplicate entries; deduplicated to one canonical serving of 350 g. 6. dietary_tags 'low_calorie' in the input cultural section is borderline given 263 kcal per 350 g serving; retained as contextual metadata only and not propagated to dietary_flags.
